name: Lake Superior North Shore
tips: Lake Superior holds enough water to flood all of North and South America under a foot of water
Let the sound of the water arrive before you try to see anything
Cold lakes have a different quality of stillness than warm ones
The shoreline here has looked roughly like this for ten thousand years

name: Grinnell Valley Headwaters Stream
tips: Glacier National Park loses roughly one glacier per year. What remains is what you're hearing
Headwaters streams are among the quietest places a river will ever be. Notice what that does to the sound
Mountain water sounds different — higher, thinner, more urgent
The air is cold and faintly metallic. That sharpness is snowmelt, not rain

name: Great Basin Glacial Valley
tips: The Great Basin has no outlet to the sea. Every drop of water here eventually evaporates
Desert silence is not the absence of sound. It is a different register entirely
This valley was carved by glaciers that melted fourteen thousand years ago
Notice how the light moves here. The air is too dry to soften it

name: Sonoran Desert Sunrise
tips: The saguaro cactus grows its first arm only after 75 years. The ones you see are older than most living people
Dawn in the desert belongs to sound. The light comes second
No other desert on earth has quite this combination of heat, spines, and unlikely abundance
The air before sunrise here is cool and faintly dusty. That warmth arriving is the rock, not the sun

name: Kuwohi (Clingmans Dome)
tips: Kuwohi is the highest point in the Great Smoky Mountains at 2,025 metres, where the elevation shifts the entire forest ecosystem
Let the horizon slow you down. You don't need to scan it all at once
Mountains this old don't rise. They settle
What looks like fog is the forest exhaling. These trees release enough moisture to wrap the valley in their own weather

name: Juniper Springs
tips: Juniper Springs discharges millions of gallons daily from the Floridan Aquifer, one of the largest freshwater systems on Earth
Let your eyes adjust before you try to understand the water. Clarity like this can feel unfamiliar at first
Clear water is not empty. It is just honest about what is there
The air carries wet stone, minerals, and the smell of subtropical vegetation warming in early sun

name: Rainy Ozarks
tips: The Ozarks are an ancient uplifted plateau where rainwater disappears into limestone and re-emerges through springs and caves
Let the rain set the pace. You don't need to move faster than it falls
Rain turns a forest into a thousand small conversations
The air is thick with wet leaves, soil, and the metallic edge of a storm that has already arrived

name: Costa Rican Rainforest
tips: Costa Rica holds around 5% of the world's biodiversity despite covering less than 0.1% of its land surface
Don't try to track every sound. Let the forest come to you instead
Nothing here is still, even when it looks like it is
The air is heavy and green-smelling. That density is not heat alone but the weight of everything growing at once

name: Amazon Jungle Thunderstorm
tips: The Amazon rainforest generates its own rainfall, recycling moisture through the atmosphere in a continuous loop
Let the storm take over your attention. You don't need to hold onto it
Thunder here doesn't interrupt the forest. It belongs to it
The air is warm and electric. That smell arriving before the rain is petrichor rising from soil that has been waiting

name: Lake Michigan Waves
tips: Lake Michigan is one of the largest freshwater lakes on Earth, where wind-driven waves can reach heights comparable to ocean surf
Let the rhythm of the waves regulate your attention. You don't need to fix it on anything else
What looks like an ocean is completely freshwater, shaped only by wind and distance, not tides
The air carries cold spray and the clean metallic edge of freshwater moving in large volume

name: Acadia Coast
tips: Acadia sits where the Atlantic meets glacier-sculpted granite, some of the oldest exposed rock on the U.S. eastern seaboard
Let the tide set the rhythm. Everything here is in slow negotiation with the sea
This coastline is not fixed ground. It is rock being continuously rewritten by wind, ice, and salt
The air carries cold Atlantic spray, pine resin, and the sharp clarity that follows moving weather

name: Adirondack Lakeside Fishing
tips: The Adirondack lakes sit inside the oldest mountain range in North America, shaped by glaciers into basins that are cold, deep, and remarkably clear
Slow your attention to the pace of the water. Fishing here is as much about waiting as it is about noticing
Stillness on a lake is never complete. It just asks you to look more carefully
The air carries pine resin and cold freshwater, with a faint metallic stillness that comes from depth and shade

name: Coastal Redwood Rainforest
tips: Coastal redwoods are the tallest trees on Earth, and they drink fog. Up to 40% of their water intake comes not from rain but from clouds moving through the canopy
Let your attention move upward slowly. These trees are measured in vertical time, not horizontal distance
Sound doesn't travel far in this forest. It gets absorbed before it ever becomes distant
The air is cool and damp, layered with salt fog, decaying wood, and the dense green smell of things that never fully dry out

name: Yanui Beach
tips: Yanui sits in a sheltered cove where limestone headlands and nearby coral reef systems absorb open-ocean energy before it reaches shore
Let the tide decide how long you stay in any one thought. Nothing here moves in straight lines
Stillness here is an effect of scale. What feels calm is movement happening just below perception
The air is warm and salt-heavy, carrying sun-heated rock, sea spray, and the faint green edge of jungle at the treeline

name: Olympic Rainforest
tips: Olympic National Park contains three distinct ecosystems — glaciated peaks, temperate rainforest, and wild Pacific coastline — all within a single protected landscape
Let the scale of the place unsettle your sense of distance. Mountains, forest, and ocean are all happening at once
What looks like separate environments is one continuous system exchanging water, air, and energy
The air shifts between salt spray, moss-heavy forest damp, and cold alpine wind depending on where you stand and which way the wind turns

name: Three Sisters Alpine Lake
tips: The Three Sisters are limestone peaks in the Canadian Rockies formed from ancient seafloor lifted by tectonic forces and carved by glaciers over millions of years
Let the evening alpine light simplify everything. Detail falls away before colour does
At this elevation, still water becomes a mirror for weather rather than landscape
The air is cold and faintly mineral, carrying pine, stone, and the memory of ice that has not fully left

name: Colorado Rockies Spring Melt
tips: Spring melt in the Rockies releases an entire winter's snowpack at once, sending stored water into rivers and streams faster than the land can absorb it
Let the sound of moving water set the pace. This is a landscape transitioning faster than it can be seen
What looks like a single river is winter returning to motion all at once
The air is cold at altitude but carries the sharp freshness of melting snow, pine, and stone newly exposed to sun

name: Yucatán Cenote
tips: Cenotes form when limestone bedrock collapses, exposing groundwater that has been filtering through porous rock for thousands of years
Let your perception adjust to the vertical shift. You are looking into a water system that runs beneath the land, not across it
Stillness here is not calm water. It is groundwater made visible, held in place by stone
The air is cool and faintly mineral, carrying wet limestone and the particular quiet of a place the sun only partially reaches

name: Laguna Las Mulitas
tips: Laguna Las Mulitas sits in the flat pampas of Argentina, where shallow lakes form in wind-shaped depressions across one of the largest grassland plains on Earth
Let the horizon widen your attention. Here, distance is the dominant feature, not elevation or enclosure
What looks like a simple lake is part of a seasonal system that expands and contracts with rainfall across the pampas
The air is open and wind-swept, carrying dry grass, soil, and the particular emptiness of long horizontal space

name: Blue Mountains
tips: The blue haze over these mountains is real and chemical. Eucalyptus forests release oils into the air that scatter light, tinting the entire distance blue
Let your attention follow depth instead of detail. This landscape is built in layers that recede rather than resolve
What looks like solid forest is a system of ridges, cliffs, and valleys carved over millions of years by slow erosion
The air carries eucalyptus vapor, dry sandstone dust, and a sharp clarity that shifts with every change in wind and light

name: Snowy Mountains Alpine Range
tips: The Snowy Mountains are Australia's highest range, and their winter snowpack feeds the Murray-Darling Basin, the river system that waters a third of the continent
Let your attention settle into scale. This is a landscape defined by exposure, not enclosure
What looks like still alpine ground is part of a seasonal cycle of storage and release that shapes entire downstream ecosystems
The air is cold, dry, and thin, carrying alpine grass, granite dust, and the quiet sharpness of high elevation

name: Colombian Jungle, Chocó
tips: The Chocó is not an extension of the Amazon. Sealed off by the Andes on one side and the Pacific on the other, it evolved in isolation — around 20% of its plant species exist nowhere else on Earth
Don't try to separate the sounds into individual sources. Let the whole thing arrive at once
The loudest rainforests are usually the most alive. Silence here would be the warning
The air is saturated and warm, carrying the smell of wet bark, decomposing leaves, and soil that has never fully dried out

name: Bahia Atlantic Forest
tips: This forest holds more life per square metre than almost anywhere on Earth
Stay with one layer of sound at a time. There is no end to what the forest is doing
A forest this dense doesn't have a background. Everything you hear is the foreground
The air is warm and thick, carrying the smell of wet soil, bark, and flowering things competing for the same small patches of light

name: Denali Tundra Hillside
tips: During the last ice age, the tundra around Denali was part of Beringia — a vast ice-free refuge connected to northeastern Asia. The plants and animals here have more in common with Siberia than with the rest of North America
Pick a point on the horizon and stay with it. At this scale, stillness takes time to arrive
Tundra wind doesn't move through things. It moves everything at once
The air is cold and almost scentless. That absence is what subarctic air smells like — space, with nothing to catch it

name: Morning in the Cloud Forest
tips: Sierra de las Minas is the source of 63 permanent rivers. They begin as fog, caught by leaves exactly like these
Watch one drop form and fall. The whole water cycle is happening at that scale
A fern doesn't wait for rain. It drinks the cloud directly
The air is cool and still, carrying wet moss, dark soil, and the mineral sharpness of stone that hasn't seen direct sun since yesterday

name: Maui Coastal Sunrise
tips: Hawaii is the most geographically isolated island chain on Earth, more than 3,200 kilometres from the nearest continent
Let the light come in stages. Sunrise here moves slower than you expect
An ocean this large doesn't have a far side you can feel. It just continues
The air is warm and salt-heavy, carrying the particular softness of trade winds that have crossed thousands of kilometres of open water before reaching land

name: Baja Beach Sounds
tips: This narrow sea contains more marine species than almost any comparable body of water on Earth
Let your eyes adjust to the water before you try to read it. Clarity this shallow takes a moment to make sense of
Volcanic rock and turquoise water shouldn't go together. Here they do
The air is dry and bright, carrying sea salt, warm basalt, and the particular sharpness of a desert meeting the ocean

name: Caribbean Blue Bay
tips: Trunk Bay is named after the leatherback sea turtles that nest here. Leatherbacks are the largest reptiles on Earth
Watch the water rather than through it. The colour itself is the first thing worth staying with
The Caribbean is not one shade of blue. It changes with depth, sand, and the angle of the sun every few metres
The air is warm and lightly salted, carried by trade winds that have moved across open Atlantic for thousands of kilometres before reaching this bay

name: San Joaquin Wetland
tips: The San Joaquin Valley once held over 800,000 acres of wetland — the largest freshwater marsh in the western United States
Let the birds set the rhythm. This is a place measured in arrivals and departures, not stillness
Wetlands don't look like much until something lands. Then everything changes
The air is flat and open, carrying cattail, still water, and the particular quiet of a valley floor with no hills to hold the wind

name: Big Bend Morning
tips: The Chisos Mountains rising from Big Bend's desert floor are a sky island — a cool, forested range so isolated by surrounding desert that their plants and animals have evolved separately from any other mountain range
Morning is a slow process, not an event
Desert silence before dawn is different from desert silence at noon. One is waiting. The other is enduring
The air is cool and bone-dry, carrying creosote, warm rock, and the faint sweetness of something blooming that you cannot see
